text: In the operating room, the surgeon uses a monopolar hook and bipolar forceps to perform adhesiolysis, carefully dissecting severe adhesions in the midline, between the stomach and liver, and between the liver and diaphragm. The procedure involves staying in the proper plane while progressively separating the capsule of the liver from the stomach.

Robotic vessel sealer is used to take down omental adhesions to the liver. Adhesions between the fundus of the stomach and the inferior surface of liver segments II and III are dissected. The surgeon mobilizes adhesions between the stomach fundus and diaphragm using a hook. The greater curvature of the stomach is dissected, with short gastric vessels ligated using the robotic vessel sealer. The gastroesplenic ligament is dissected, and dissection continues distally to the origin of the right gastroepiploic vessels, which are ligated with a hemo-lock clip and vessel sealer. The first portion of the duodenum is mobilized and transected using a stapler.

Following the transection of the duodenum, the staple line is reinforced with figure-of-eight sutures using PDS-III-O. The surgeon performs a prophylactic cholecystectomy on the notably distended gallbladder. The pedicle of the left gastric artery is divided while preserving the aberrant left hepatic artery.
The surgeon evaluates the blood perfusion of the proximal gastric stump using indocyanine green (ICG) imaging. After confirming adequate perfusion, the stomach is divided with a surgical stapler.

The surgeon prepares the Roux-en-Y jejunal loop by dividing the jejunum with a stapler and constructs a side-to-side enteroenterostomy. The root of the Roux-en-Y and the enterotomy are closed with a running suture of PDS-III-O. The distal jejunal loop is positioned in the upper quadrants, and a gastrojejunal side-to-side anastomosis is performed using a stapler. The small enterotomy is closed with a running suture of PDS-III-O, and a short stump of the jejunal loop is resected with a stapler due to congestion.
